issue_title: Playback does not work more than once with HackRF
issue_body:
_Please use this template for bug reports. If you have a feature request or question just delete everything and write as you like._
## Expected Behavior
Sending with HackRF should work more than one time...

## Actual Behavior
Sending with HackRF only works once. After sending a waveform again the progress bar does not appear and nothing seems to be sent. Resetting the HackRF sometimes helps to make it work again...

Unplugging HackRF and restarting URH always solves the issue. After that most of the times sending works only once again....

## Steps to Reproduce the Problem

  1. Record with e.g. 8Mhz
  2. Playback the recording
  3. Stop the playback and press the play button again -> No progress bar and sending does not work

## Platform Specifications

  - Python Version:
  - Operating System: Windows 10
  - Version of URH: 1.8.12
  - URH was installed:  via ```.msi```

issue_title: TornadoSession.validate enters endless loop upon failure
issue_body:
Not completely sure what is going on here, but when `TornadoSession.validate` is called with a bad password it fails with an exception which is fine except that something is left on the ioloop that eats the CPU alive.  The following script will show the problem when you run it with a DSN that is valid except for the password:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
#
import logging
import signal
import sys

import queries
from tornado import gen, ioloop


@gen.coroutine
def main():
    logger = logging.getLogger('main')
    for dsn in sys.argv[1:]:
        logger.info('starting session with dsn=%s', dsn)
        try:
            session = queries.TornadoSession(dsn)
            yield session.validate()
            logger.info('validated')
        except:
            logger.exception('FAILURE FOR %s', dsn)

    while True:
        yield gen.sleep(10)


def sig_handler(*args):
    logging.info('signal handler invoked with %r', args)
    iol = ioloop.IOLoop.instance()
    iol.add_callback_from_signal(iol.stop)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    if len(sys.argv) == 1:
        print('Usage:', sys.argv[0], 'DSN [DSN...]')
        sys.exit(1)

    logging.basicConfig(
        level=logging.DEBUG,
        format='[%(process)d] %(levelname)1.1s - %(name)s: %(message)s')
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, sig_handler)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, sig_handler)
    iol = ioloop.IOLoop.instance()
    iol.add_future(main(), lambda _: None)
    iol.start()
```

When given a valid DSN it will validate the session and sleep until you kill with Ctrl+C.  Give it an invalid password and it will report and error and go into the same sleep cycle with something like the following log:
```
(env) Daves-MBP/queries-play% ./validate.py 'postgresql://postgres:secret2@127.0.0.1:32770/postgres'
[20562] I - main: starting session with dsn=postgresql://postgres:secret2@127.0.0.1:32770/postgres
[20562] D - queries.pool: Creating Pool: ea2750c53184e9ddec80659446cbe6fa (60/25)
[20562] D - queries.tornado_session: Creating a new connection for ea2750c53184e9ddec80659446cbe6fa
[20562] E - main: FAILURE FOR postgresql://postgres:secret2@127.0.0.1:32770/postgres
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./validate.py", line 18, in main
    yield session.validate()
  File "/Users/daveshawley/Source/python/queries-play/env/lib/python3.4/site-packages/tornado/gen.py", line 1015, in run
    value = future.result()
  File "/Users/daveshawley/Source/python/queries-play/env/lib/python3.4/site-packages/tornado/concurrent.py", line 237, in result
    raise_exc_info(self._exc_info)
  File "<string>", line 3, in raise_exc_info
  File "/Users/daveshawley/Source/python/queries-play/env/lib/python3.4/site-packages/queries/tornado_session.py", line 460, in _poll_connection
    state = self._connections[fd].poll()
psycopg2.OperationalError: FATAL:  password authentication failed for user "postgres"

```

Now run top or similar process monitor and you'll notice that the process is consuming an entire core.  It is not chewing through memory or stranding sockets (verified by `lsof`).  It is simply looping very hard on something.  I ran `sudo dtrace -p 20562 -n 'syscall:::entry'` to see what was going on and it looks like it might be failing to handle a socket error and retrying the connection sequence repeatedly.  The dtrace output settles to:
```
  4    883           workq_kernreturn:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  6    873                     kevent:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  0    333                     select:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  6    873                     kevent:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
```
repeatedly.  There are no calls to IO traps so I don't think that it is trying a new connection.  It looks like it is simply looping repeatedly.

issue_title: Browser gets frozen when a command outputs too much data
issue_body:
When we type a command that outputs too much (or unlimited) data, for example ```find /proc``` or ```yes```, the browser (Firefox, even Chrome) will be very slow or gets frozen. This performance issue is due to the algorithm you use when rendering output on screen : you first write the entire data into a buffer and then you display it. This means : the data is read twice.

I know this is not quite simple, but I wonder is it possible to do better to get higher performance so that the browser will not get frozen at least.

issue_title: Disable automatic coercion of physical quantities
issue_body:
Hi, I was looking into the Pint package for adding physical quantities to a simulation package. For checking model consistency and educational purposes the use of physical quantities is great. However, the downside is that they impose a considerable penalty on the computational performance of the model. So for bulk simulations of a tested model you would like to switch off the use of physical quantities. The latter could be implemented by importing a dummy package where all the units are defined as 1.0.
However, this approach would fail if somewhere in the model code Pint carries out an implicit conversion of quantities. For example when summing two variables with quantities kg and g, Pint would silently do the conversion. Instead, I would like to see that pint raises an error and forces you to do an explicit conversion of g to kg in the code itself.

I was therefore wondering if there is an easy way to implement a setting in Pint that forces this behaviour?

issue_title: Extract mapped genome sequence from mappy API
issue_body: I am using the `mappy` API with the sole end goal of extracting the genomic sequence for a read mapping. Currently, I am using [`pyfaidx`](https://github.com/mdshw5/pyfaidx) to take the mapped coordinates from the `mappy.Alignment` object and extract the associated genomic sequence. This seems like a bit of extra work since the sequence in theory may be extract-able from the `mappy.Aligner` or `mappy.Alignment`objects (though there are likely reasons this may not be true). This would also allow my code not to load the same reference fasta file twice (once for mapping in `mappy` and the second time for randomly extracting genome sequence). Is there access to this right now via the python API (maybe undocumented)? Or would it be possible to add this feature either directly or indirectly to the `mappy.Alignment` object?

In [11]: @lrudecorator(100)

   ....:

issue_title: Comparable/hashable (d)rectangles in Python
issue_body:
As of know, `dlib.drectangle` and `dlib.rectangle` instances are not comparable (nor hash-stable):

```python
>>> import dliv
>>> x1 = dlib.drectangle(0, 1, 2, 3)
>>> x2 = dliv.drectangle(0, 1, 2, 3)
>>> assert x1 == x2, 'Rectangles differ'
AssertionError: Rectangle differ
>>> assert hash(x1) == hash(x2), 'Hashes differ'
AssertionError: Hashes differ
```

Therefore, they cannot be used as dictionary keys for instance, nor in a set.
Solving this issue can be solved by implementing `__hash__` and `__eq__` methods in Python API.

I am willing to help but am not familiar with interfacing C and Python API so I don't really know where to start...

issue_title: uniq functions time complexity
issue_body:
This implementation of uniq with the usage of arrays as the collection of seen items has clearly a (worst case) time complexity of O(n^2). However in case of input items are hashable, a linear implementation can be achieved by using a hashset here instead. Please make the method more efficient or at least add some warnings about this in the docstrings.

https://github.com/dgilland/pydash/blob/cb103ca6363cc5d497c116b11534aa18bf177a2f/pydash/arrays.py#L2291

```
import time
from datetime import datetime
import pydash as py_
s = datetime.now()
py_.uniq(range(10000))
t = datetime.now()
print(t-s)
```
`0:00:42.274382`

issue_title: depth argument of Cell.get_labels() is confusing
issue_body:
I'm referring to the following code segment.

```python
    def get_labels(self, depth=None):
        labels = libCopy.deepcopy(self.labels)
        if depth is None or depth >= 0:
            for element in self.elements:
                if isinstance(element, CellReference):
                    labels.extend(
                        element.get_labels(None if depth is None else depth -
                                           1))
                elif isinstance(element, CellArray):
                    labels.extend(
                        element.get_labels(None if depth is None else depth -
                                           1))
        return labels
```

Passing in depth=0, one gets labels from the top-level cell, plus labels from the first-level child elements.
In order to get labels from the top-level cell only, one has to pass in depth=-1. 

This behavior was confusing to my colleagues. After reading the code, I think it's better to change one line:
```python
        if depth is None or depth > 0:
```

Because the base prompt changes, netmiko errors out when switching VDCs:

```
C:\Users\chart2>python
Python 3.6.4 (v3.6.4:d48eceb, Dec 19 2017, 06:04:45) [MSC v.1900 32 bit (Intel)] on win32
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

>>> target_con = ConnectHandler(**nx_device)
>>> target_con.send_command("switchto vdc RTP-AGG1")

    search_pattern))
OSError: Search pattern never detected in send_command_expect: N7K\-A\-Admin\#
```

At the moment, this can be handled by the user using the `expect_string` parameter of BaseConnection.send_command() or BaseConnection.send_command_expect() when sending either `switchto vdc VDC_NAME` or `switchback`, as shown below.

```
>>> target_con.send_command("switchto vdc RTP-AGG1", expect_string="RTP-AGG1#")
'Cisco Nexus Operating System (NX-OS) Software\nTAC support: http://www.cisco.com/tac\nCopyright (c) 2002-2017, Cisco Systems, Inc. ''
```

However, precedent for changing the base prompt on Cisco devices where necessary has been set by cisco_asa_ssh.py for ASA devices configured in multi-context mode. There should be a similar implementation for handling VDCs on NX-OS devices.

issue_title: Abdera child nodes incorrectly placed in same dictionaries
issue_body:
I seemed to have encountered a bug with the Abdera conversion logic, specifically how it converts children:
```
for child in children:
            child_data = self.data(child)
            if (count[child.tag] == 1
                    and len(children_list) > 1
                    and isinstance(children_list[-1], dict)):
                # Merge keys to existing dictionary
                children_list[-1].update(child_data)
            else:
                # Add additional text
                children_list.append(self.data(child))
```

When given the following XML data:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<Data
   version="9.0"
   xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
   xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="comp.xsd">
    <Airport
      country="Samoa"
      city="Apia"
      name="Faleolo Intl"
      lat="-13.8296668231487"
      lon="-171.997166723013"
      alt="17.678M"
      ident="NSFA"
      >
      <Services>
    	 <Fuel
    		type="JETA"
    		availability="YES"/>
      </Services>
      <Tower
    	 lat="-13.8320958986878"
    	 lon="-171.998676359653"
    	 alt="0.0M">
      </Tower>
      <Runway
    	 lat="-13.8300792127848"
    	 lon="-172.008545994759"
    	 alt="17.678M"
    	 surface="ASPHALT"
    	 heading="89.3199996948242"
    	 length="2999.23M"
    	 width="45.11M"
    	 number="08"
    	 designator="NONE">
      </Runway>
    </Airport>
</Data>
```
I receive the following output from `ab.data(xml_file)`:
```
{
    "Data": {
        "attributes": {
            "version": 9.0,
            "{http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance}noNamespaceSchemaLocation": "comp.xsd"
        },
        "children": [
            {
                "Airport": {
                    "attributes": {
                        "country": "Samoa",
                        "city": "Apia",
                        "name": "Faleolo Intl",
                        "lat": -13.8296668231487,
                        "lon": -171.997166723013,
                        "alt": "17.678M",
                        "ident": "NSFA"
                    },
                    "children": [
                        {
                            "Services": {
                                "Fuel": {
                                    "attributes": {
                                        "type": "JETA",
                                        "availability": "YES"
                                    }
                                }
                            }
                        },
                        {
                            "Tower": {
                                "attributes": {
                                    "lat": -13.8320958986878,
                                    "lon": -171.998676359653,
                                    "alt": "0.0M"
                                }
                            },
                            "Runway": {
                                "attributes": {
                                    "lat": -13.8300792127848,
                                    "lon": -172.008545994759,
                                    "alt": "17.678M",
                                    "surface": "ASPHALT",
                                    "heading": 89.3199996948242,
                                    "length": "2999.23M",
                                    "width": "45.11M",
                                    "number": 8,
                                    "designator": "NONE"
                                }
                            }
                        }
                    ]
                }
            }
        ]
    }
}

```

The first child node, `Services`, is placed into a separate dictionary, while the rest of the children are placed in the same dictionary. Because multiple tags with the same keys can be present in XML, all children should probably be placed in separate dictionaries.

I'm working on a PR for this, but if you happen to no what the problem is, let me know.

issue_title: Import failed TextBlob in hexchat
Problems after upgrading 4.0.1 to 4.0.2
issue_body:
After upgrading from 4.0.1 to 4.0.2, in some cases, `isbntools` stop work!

**Solution**
1. try `pip uninstall isbntools` (or `sudo pip uninstall isbntools`) and reinstall `isbntools`.
2. if the above doesn't work, try to see where python has installed `isbntools` and **delete** all files
    `isbntools*` **and** the directory `isbntools`. Reinstall `isbntools` again, this should solve the problem.

issue_title: Merging arrays of dicts?
issue_body:
Hi, I had some yaml files with an array of dicts in it, and I'm confused how hiyapyco is supposed to behave in this scenario. Not sure if it's a bug or not.

base.yml

```yaml
key_one: 1
key_two: 2
array_of_dicts:
- dict_key_one: a
  dict_key_two: b
  dict_key_three: c
- dict_key_one: a1
  dict_key_two: b1
  dict_key_three: c1
```

layer.yml

```yaml
key_two: 2222
array_of_dicts:
- dict_key_one: a2
  dict_key_two: b2
  dict_key_three: c2
```

merge.py

```py
import hiyapyco

CONF = hiyapyco.load("base.yml", "layer.yml", method=hiyapyco.METHOD_MERGE)

result:

```yaml
key_one: 1
key_two: 2222
array_of_dicts:

I thought it would have produced:

```yaml
key_one: 1
key_two: 2222
array_of_dicts:
- {dict_key_one: a, dict_key_two: b, dict_key_three: c}
- {dict_key_one: a1, dict_key_two: b1, dict_key_three: c1}
- {dict_key_one: a2, dict_key_two: b2, dict_key_three: c2}
```

Is this a bug?

issue_title: Refactor ping handling out of StanzaStream
issue_body:
This should help with a proper implementation of #96.

The idea is to refactor the whole ping handling out of the StanzaStream into separate classes (for XMPP Ping, SM Ping and possibly other implementations). StanzaStream must then provide the appropriate callbacks, including for reception and sending of ``<r/>`` and ``<a/>`` elements (while still handling SM itself).

Seems like 745051ee3e239d62210e52668105e30214dd7fb7 broke again with the final flake8 3.0 release :worried: 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/florian/proj/qutebrowser/git/.tox/flake8/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flake8/plugins/manager.py", line 159, in load_plugin
    self._load(verify_requirements)
  File "/home/florian/proj/qutebrowser/git/.tox/flake8/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flake8/plugins/manager.py", line 137, in _load
    self._plugin = resolve()
  File "/home/florian/proj/qutebrowser/git/.tox/flake8/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2235, in resolve
    module = __import__(self.module_name, fromlist=['__name__'], level=0)
  File "/home/florian/proj/qutebrowser/git/.tox/flake8/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flake8_tuple.py", line 8, in <module>
    from flake8.engine import pep8
ImportError: No module named 'flake8.engine'
```

It looks like you should probably use something like:

```
try:
    import pycodestyle as pep8
except ImportError:
    import pep8
```

as well as using `flake8-polyfill` (according to the [flake8 docs](http://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/plugin-development/cross-compatibility.html#standard-in-handling-on-flake8-2-5-2-6-and-3)) to get correct behaviour with all versions...

issue_title: Configuration options should be optional
issue_body: If I only use the "-" or "0" options in my project, and don't configure the `releases_issue_uri` and `releases_release_uri` options in my `conf.py` file, I get the following stack trace: ``` Exception occurred: File "...\.tox\docs\lib\site-packages\releases\__init__.py", line 129, in release_nodes link = '{1}'.format(uri, text) UnboundLocalError: local variable 'uri' referenced before assignment ``` This is not super user friendly. If the options are required (looks like it from the docs), shouldn't we try to enforce the presence? Also, since it's possible not to use these at all, can we make it optional? I understand that if someone uses the issue numbers but doesn't set the options, then maybe we'd want a warning or something, but crashing like this is probably not what you had in mind. I'm willing to send in a patch if you're interested. Just let me know what you want as the final behavior.

issue_title: Build CEF on Mac without the tcmalloc memory allocator
issue_body:
Currently builds for Mac are with tcmalloc global hook enabled. This means that the CEF Python library needs to be imported the very first before any other libraries like for example wxPython.

There was a similar tcmalloc issue for Linux, see Issue #73 for details.

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `czarek.t...@gmail.com` on 9 Jan 2015 at 12:11

See this shell output

```
~

Using Anaconda API: https://api.anaconda.org

   + 1.6.0

   + v1.6.2

To install this package with conda run:
     conda install --channel https://conda.anaconda.org/Henryiii plumbum
                                                                                                                                                               
~  
❯ conda install --channel https://conda.anaconda.org/Henryiii plumbum=v1.6.2
```

Note the line

         Henryiii/plumbum          |    1.6.0 | conda           | linux-64, win-32, win-64, linux-32, osx-64

It says the latest version is `1.6.0` (which is incorrect, latest is `1.6.2` at time of writing)

Then note the lines:

```
Versions:
   + 1.6.0
   + v1.6.1
   + v1.6.2
```

I suspect it's the trailing `v` causing trouble.

For others who want a temporary fix. You can actually run

    ❯ conda install --channel https://conda.anaconda.org/Henryiii plumbum=v1.6.2

And it will work. :)

issue_title: Unnamed nodes should make up useful names for themselves
issue_body:
When you print a node, you get something like this:

```
<Node called "char_range" matching "c-a">
    <Node called "class_char" matching "c">
        <RegexNode matching "c">
    <Node matching "-">
    <Node called "class_char" matching "a">
        <RegexNode matching "a">
```

Named nodes get names, but unnamed ones (like the one matching "-") don't. Especially when debugging—for instance, ending up in visit_generic and not knowing what node triggered that dispatch—it would be helpful if nodes would identify themselves by their rule expression: `<Node "class_item*" matching "abc">`. Then we take the "called" out of the named nodes, and everything is frighteningly consistent.

issue_title: mypy==0.620 compatibility issue
issue_body:
**Versions**

- `Python 3.6.5`

- `mypy==0.620+dev.bc0b551d8df2baadc44f0c3b0b801fcc12119658`

- `dataclasses==0.6`

**What's wrong**

```
$ cat f2.py 
from dataclasses import dataclass
print('works!')
$ python f2.py 
works!
$ mypy f2.py 
f2.py:1: error: Cannot find module named 'dataclasses'
f2.py:1: note: (Perhaps setting MYPYPATH or using the "--ignore-missing-imports" flag would help)
```

So the natural way is to use MYPYPATH to point to the specific package, `dataclasses` in this case. The problem is `dataclasses` package is installed directly in `site-packages` (`virtual_env_path/lib/python3.6/site-packages/dataclasses.py` in my case), and it's an error (and a bad idea) to pass the whole `site-packages` to MYPYPATH. 

When I manually move the file from `/site-packages/dataclasses.py` to `/site-packages/dataclasses/dataclasses.py` and pass the path to MYPYPATH it's working as expected: no mypy error. Unfortunately, this is not the solution I can use.

I reported this issue on mypy page (https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/5342) and was told that:

> The backport package can fix this by including stubs (presumably the same ones that are in typeshed) and marking itself as typed by using `py.typed`.

Can anything be done about it? I cannot migrate to Python 3.7 yet and would benefit much from mypy actually understanding dataclasses.

issue_title: Feature request: `ia download` should (optionally?) use range requests to resume downloads (and retries seem broken)
issue_body:
According to `ia help download`, it defaults to retrying 5 times.

These command outputs don't seem to show any retries: https://gist.github.com/ethus3h/b9959869b9b00768cbff1a358efb217a https://gist.github.com/ethus3h/0722f52f5918460e170b5e06628243a8

I'm in a rural area, and have a rather unreliable Internet connection. (It usually goes down every few hours, and can range from dying every couple of minutes when it's bad, to lasting maybe half a day when it's doing well. Often when it goes down, it will be entirely unavailable for anywhere from a few minutes to many hours, as it requires a human to notice and reset the equipment.)

When the connection goes down, `ia download` does not seem to retry, and will exit with an error status (I use a [wrapper shell script](https://github.com/ethus3h/ember-shared/blob/master/scripts/ember_bash_setup#L394) to catch this and restart it). `ia download` does not seem to keep the already-downloaded portion of the file and use a HTTP range request to resume the previous try, either.

Consequently, downloading a moderately large file (e.g. the one in the logs above, ~6GB) usually takes several days and tens or hundreds of GB of bandwidth since it keeps going back to the beginning until it gets lucky and the connection lasts long enough for the file to get through.

This doesn't seem very efficient, so I'm writing this feature request.

Thanks for considering it!

issue_title: Including non-builtin class in names allows end user to snoop
issue_body:
If the user (source of untrusted code) can get access to a non-builtin function, such as a method defined on a non-builtin class, they can access the functions `func_globals` or `__globals__` property and see everything defined in that function's module.  This includes other imported modules and often leads to access to `sys`, and by extension, `sys.modules`.  This could allow an attacker to find sensitive data (usernames, passwords, PII) by crawling through the list of modules.  Even if the sensitive information is not defined at top level, inspection of function objects will reveal any constants or default arguments used anywhere in the running system.

At a minimum, attribute lookup for attributes beginning with `_` should be denied (plus the special case in python2 to deny func_*).  Ideally, attribute lookup should have a configurable max-depth, or a whitelist of allowed types and classes.

issue_title: Integration tests fail for Pull Requests in Travis
issue_body:
See e.g. https://travis-ci.org/jantman/awslimitchecker/jobs/72610593#L711

When Travis checks out a pull request, it checks out the merge of the PR to master, i.e. ``git fetch origin +refs/pull/45/merge``. The git-based integration tests (``Test_AGPLVersionChecker_Acceptance.test_install_git_e_dirty``, ``Test_AGPLVersionChecker_Acceptance.test_install_git_e`` and ``Test_AGPLVersionChecker_Acceptance.test_install_git``) attempt to install ``awslimitchecker`` from GitHub using the commit hash of the current git checkout that the tests exist in - which, in this case, is the merge hash, which doesn't exist yet in the repo.

Options that I can think of:
(1) don't just check out whatever hash the test repo is at, if it doesn't exist in origin.
(2) Look at the value of the ``TRAVIS_PULL_REQUEST`` environment variable, and do something different in the integration tests if we're testing a PR (such as installing ``+refs/pull/$TRAVIS_PULL_REQUEST/merge`` instead of the SHA).

issue_title: Clean partial_pipeline session in auth_process view
issue_body:
In case that partial_pipeline is saved in session it making a race condition and various bugs depending on user pipeline functions. It looks like that the root cause is the existence of the session data.

One example is with a form for additional data in a middle of pipeline. Similar like in example app. If a user does not complete the registration process and wants to authenticate with a different backend, there is a race condition (and/or one bug related to multiple keyword argument `pipeline_index` in calling backend.continue_pipeline in auth_complete in views.py).

It would be expected to clean partial_pipeline session on starting new authentication. One of the solutions is to add:

```python
    try:
        del request.session[setting('SOCIAL_AUTH_PARTIAL_PIPELINE_KEY',
                                    'partial_pipeline')]
    except KeyError:
        pass
```

to `auth_process` in views.py.

Best,
Janos

issue_title: PHash() is not throwing out high-frequency image data.
issue_body:
As of commit:  https://github.com/JohannesBuchner/imagehash/commit/481148afa79eca6c80b853ecccabc128d7cef514

The phash() and phash_simple() algorithms are no longer tossing out high-frequency image data.

The rest of this issue description is the same as the comment I wrote on that commit:

----

On average_hash and dhash the hash_size parameter is used to indicate how small to make the sub-sampled image, which directly becomes the number of bits in the resulting hash value.

But phash (and phash_simple) there's two steps of subsampling, not one. The first subsample is when we reduce the image, and the second subsample is when we select which DCT coefficients to use. The second subsample determines the number of bits in the resulting hash value.

These two steps really need to be done at different sizes. The whole reason phash works is because it tosses out high-frequency image data, that which is most likely to be different in similar-looking images, and it keeps only the low-frequency data. This happened in the past because we downsized the image to 32x32 pixels (subsample 1) which generated 32x32 DCT coefficients, and then we kept only 8x8 of the DCT coefficients (subsample 2).

But after this commit, the algorithm is keeping ALL the DCT coefficients. It downsizes to 8x8 pixels and keeps all 8x8 coefficients. It no longer throws out high-frequency data.

This means that similar images that would have hashed to similar phash values are now much more likely to differ by more bits in their phash values, because that high-frequency data is being included.

(You can also easily show that phash_simple is returning fewer bits than expected because it's basically running off the edge of the dct matrix)

Keys should be frozenset, so that it can be more useful

issue_title: Decompression fails if the compressed content is an empty bytestring
issue_body:
```
>>> zstd.ZstdDecompressor().decompress(zstd.ZstdCompressor(write_content_size=True).compress(b''))
ZstdError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-11-89c4c9bb5b8c> in <module>()
----> 1 zstd.ZstdDecompressor().decompress(zstd.ZstdCompressor(write_content_size=True).compress(b''))

ZstdError: input data invalid or missing content size in frame header
``` 

The cause is that there is no differentiation between a lack of a frame header and a frame header indicating content size = 0.

```
>>> zstd.ZstdCompressor(write_content_size=True).compress(b'') == zstd.ZstdCompressor(write_content_size=False).compress(b'')

True
```

Obviously this is a corner case, but one of the fundamental properties of a compression library should be that `decompress(compress(x)) == x` for all x.

issue_title: Returns only the first chunk on a file downloading
issue_body:
https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/blob/9f07eab9c2f86c3d911ec9f6b0f45dca36626bd1/storages/backends/apache_libcloud.py#L154

When django tries to retrieve any file more than 5mb (https://github.com/apache/libcloud/blob/trunk/libcloud/storage/drivers/s3.py#L78), the only first chunk returns.

issue_title: clipperScale
issue_body:
It's currently set to 100000.  This is not ideal.  It has more rounding error that it has to.  For example, using this scale, 18 / 3 will NOT result in 6:
```
>>> s = 100000
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s))
6.000000000000001
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s)) == 6
False
```

Whereas if you use a power of two, rounding will be minimized.  I suggest 1<<16, to be close to your existing number.

```
>>> s = 1<<16
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s))
6.0
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s)) == 6
True
```

issue_title: Split C400 by type
issue_body:
To eliminate confusion between dict and set comprehensions, our style guideline is that we do not use set literals or comprehensions, and instead use the explicit `set()` constructor.

For this reason, I'd like to be able to ignore `C400` warnings about set comprehensions, but keep them for dict/list comprehensions.

What do you think about splitting `C400` into 3 separate warnings? This appears to be a fairly common sort of thing to do, i.e. if you see `E127` and `E128`, they are both "you indented it wrong", but in opposite directions, to allow more fine-tuning.

Would you accept a PR for this if I implemented it?

With ATContentTypes, there used to be a message for editors when they view a Link: "You see this page because you have permission to edit this link. Others will be immediately redirected to the link's target URL."

This text is there in the [plone.app.contenttypes template](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.contenttypes/blob/1.2.18/plone/app/contenttypes/browser/templates/link.pt#L10) but it tries to fill slot `header` which does not exist.  So it is never shown.

This is on both 5.0 and 5.1.

I am about to fix it, but wanted to document it first.

issue_title: Annoy less resilient to id holes than before
issue_body:
Hey @erikbern, 

TL;DR: `1.9.4` is a lot less robust to holes in the id space than `1.3.2`.

--

After upgrading annoy to `1.9.4` for DW, we got a pretty significant drop in metrics. 

After investigations, it seems that compared to older versions of annoy, the latest version is not very robust to holes in the IDs (which we have for DW because of some multistage filtering logic). 

In an experiment where I compared building a tree without holes (id: 0 to n-1) with 30% of holes in the ids, I found that annoy `1.3.2` got a 3% drop in perf (cos sim of nn) where annoy `1.9.4` perf dropped by 36%.

Here are the numbers for mean cos sim of a bunch of nn queries:
```
	old_annoy	new_annoy	old_holes_annoy	new_holes_annoy
count	5000.000000	5000.000000	3499.000000	3520.000000
mean	0.135056	0.132766	0.140376	0.180412
std	0.013602	0.013103	0.017448	0.017294
min	0.099506	0.096045	0.091715	0.130184
25%	0.125277	0.123374	0.128233	0.168452
50%	0.134288	0.131794	0.138153	0.179342
75%	0.143350	0.141121	0.150459	0.191101
max	0.197450	0.189082	0.218467	0.257536
```

(If you want the code to reproduce this I can open source it)

Were you aware of this issue?

* If the character value is greater than the line length it defaults back to the
* line length.
```

However, validation in `jedi` causes an exception to be thrown.

```
558.368137 on 1: Dropping message '2017-11-21 17:22:37,470 UTC - ERROR - jsonrpc.manager - API Exception: {'type': 'ValueError', 'args': ('`column` parameter is not in a valid range.',), 'message': '`column` parameter is not in a valid range.'}

    position=position
  File "/home/w0rp/Downloads/python-ls-project/ve/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyls/_utils.py", line 52, in race_hooks
    for impl, result in pool.imap_unordered(_apply, impls, chunksize=1):

  File "/home/w0rp/Downloads/python-ls-project/ve/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyls/_utils.py", line 47, in _apply

issue_title: Produce a `bcr-stats` output for the BCR calculator
issue_body:
The benefit-cost ratio (BCR) calculator, if used with a non-trivial logic-tree currently, produces outputs for each of the individual branches realized in the calculation. These are saved in the datastore with the name `bcr-rlzs`. Unlike all of the other hazard and risk calculators however, mean and quantile results are currently not provided.

The mean calculated across all realized branches should be provided for all BCR calculations, and stored with the name `bcr-stats`, similar to the other risk calculators. For consistency with the other calculators, the calculation of the mean results can be skipped by specifying `mean_bcr_maps = false` in the job configuration file.

Users of the BCR calculator should also be able to request quantiles calculated across all realized branches, by using the parameter `quantile_bcr_maps` in the job configuration file to specify a set of quantiles (eg. `quantile_bcr_maps = 0.15, 0.50, 0.85`).

| 
|     .

| diff --git a/b b/b
| new file mode 100644

  Author: me
  Date:   Tue Apr 17 18:12:36 2018 +0200
  
      .
  
  diff --git a/a b/a
  new file mode 100644
  index 0000000..e69de29
```

I'm running the following piece of Python code in that folder:

```python
import git

rp = git.Repo('.')

origin = rp.remotes.origin
target_head = origin.refs['master']
source_head = origin.refs['feature']

source_head.checkout(force=True)

index = git.IndexFile.from_tree(rp, target_head, source_head)
merge_commit = index.commit('merge', parent_commits=(target_head.commit, source_head.commit))
```

The resulting graph looks ok:
```
*   commit 16a40fdbd397cd49d13b53d16e2433f53c04239f (HEAD)
|\  Merge: 5ae778f 9be704f
| | Author: me
| | Date:   Wed Apr 18 10:58:40 2018 +0200
| |

| | 
| |     .
| |

| | new file mode 100644
| | index 0000000..e69de29
| | 
* | commit 5ae778f72bf14616a6129c7e4c83478daa30770b (origin/master, master)
|/  Author: me
|   Date:   Tue Apr 17 18:13:12 2018 +0200
|   
|       .

| 
* commit f3f942edc12744965bfe91402b86c1e8b2904f42
  Author: me
  Date:   Tue Apr 17 18:12:36 2018 +0200
  
      .
  
  diff --git a/a b/a
  new file mode 100644
  index 0000000..e69de29
```

However, the file `b` (which was added in commit `5ae778f72bf14616a6129c7e4c83478daa30770b` on `master` after `feature` was branched from `master`) is not in the tree of the resulting merge commit. So it seems like all commits between merge base and `master` get reverted this way.

```bash
$ git diff 16a40fdbd397cd49d13b53d16e2433f53c04239f 5ae778f72bf14616a6129c7e4c83478daa30770b

diff --git a/c b/b
similarity index 100%
rename from c
rename to b
```

(In this case, the "removal" was interpreted as a rename because both files are empty.)

The whole merging works if I'm calling `merge_tree` on the normal repo index. But according to the documentation, `from_tree` should do the same with an in-memory (or temporary) index.

issue_title: OFFSET and LIMIT are applied in the wrong order
issue_body:
Example:

```python
    def test_select_with_limit_and_offset(self):
        q1 = Query.from_('abc').select('foo')[10:10]

        self.assertEqual('SELECT "foo" FROM "abc" OFFSET 10 LIMIT 10', str(q1))
```

The query should actually be `SELECT "foo" FROM "abc" LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10`

issue_title: maintainer= generates AUTHOR: line in PKG-INFO
issue_body:
This seems like a deliberate choice, but it seems that at the moment the `Author:` metadata is pulled preferentially from `maintainer=`, and `Maintainer:` is never actually generated.

The implementation is [here](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/d10a1bb476db25dc888b5b4aad9d81ffa245ea30/setuptools/dist.py#L55), and the implementation of `get_contact` that prioritizes `maintainer` is [here](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/8837dd092fe5ad5184889104e8036811ed839f98/Lib/distutils/dist.py#L1119).

I suspect that this is an artifact of the fact that [PEP 314](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0314/#author-optional) does not provide a `Maintainer` field, and that was a later addition in [PEP 345](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/#maintainer-email-optional)?

I can make a PR for this, but there is an implementation question. The options I see right now are:

1. Maintain the current behavior and simply *add* a "Maintainer" field. This is the most "backwards compatible" way to do things.
2. Change the behavior so that `author=` is given preference over `maintainer=` when generating the `AUTHOR` field, but still use `maintainer` if no `author` is specified. Additionally always add the `Maintainer` field if `maintainer=` is present.
3. Change the behavior so that *only* `author=` generates the `Author` field and *only* `maintainer=` generates the `Maintainer:` field. This is the least backwards-incompatible change.

I suspect that the choice of these probably should come down to how PyPI and Warehouse handle the metadata. If both PyPI and Warehouse are able to handle an either/or Author/Maintainer field, then I suggest that the best option is 3. Basically the fallback logic should be on the metadata consumer side, not on the metadata *producer* side.

OSError: Command /root/foo/bin/python2.7 - setuptools pip wheel failed with error code 1
```

Tracking this down in virtualenv.py, this is caused by ``install_wheel()`` running an embedded script using a munged pythonpath (assembled [here](https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/blob/15.0.3/virtualenv.py#L837-L838) and containing only the paths of the wheels found by ``find_wheels()``). The problem with this is that the embedded python script which ``install_wheel()`` wants to run imports tempfile, which eventually needs to import ``_io.so`` from ``/usr/lib64/python2.7/lib-dynload``.

I was able to get it fixed with [this commit](https://github.com/terminalmage/virtualenv/commit/b989c3ba23cfbcd976932fbce43d529556de4b25), but rather than just opening a PR I thought I'd post my findings here and see if there isn't a more elegant solution from someone more experienced with this codebase.

issue_title: Rescale intensity returns only a float given an array
issue_body:
## Description

I start with a numpy `array` that has `array.shape == (1, 1)` and `array.dtype == np.float64`. Let's define an example `array = np.array([[0]], dtype=np.float64)`.

If I then pass this float64 array as the first argument of `exposure.rescale_intensity`, the function gives `np.float64(0.0)` as an output instead of the expected `np.array([[0.0]], dtype=np.float64)`.

This is caused by [line 298 of exposure.py](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/b02e2ce7014f245ff20d692519f2c36ed4bf8269/skimage/exposure/exposure.py#L298)

We should replace this line's `dtype(image * (omax - omin) + omin)` with this line:

```python
np.array(image * (omax - omin) + omin, dtype=dtype)
```

When called as a function, `np.float64` has the unexpected behavior that this is true: 

```python
np.float64(np.array([[0]])) == np.float64(0.0)
```

Therefore, contrary to expected behavior, this is false:

```python
type(np.float64(np.array([[0]]))) == np.ndarray
```

I've raised an [issue at numpy/numpy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/10995), but it appears this behavior will not be corrected given that the `np.float64(array)` syntax is undocumented. I believe all code that depends on numpy should move away from using `np.float64(*)` as an array constructor. It is always safer to use `np.array(*, dtype=np.float64)`.

## Way to reproduce

```python
import numpy as np
import skimage.exposure

array = np.array([[0]], dtype=np.float64)
output = skimage.exposure.rescale_intensity(array, (0, 1))
assert type(output) == np.ndarray #assertion fails
```
Iff `dtype == np.float64`, instead of `[[0.0]]`, rescale_intensity returns`0.0`

- ubuntu 16.04 LTS
- python 3.6.4
- scikit-image 0.13.1

issue_title: api: make the FormatChecker feature of jsonschema accessible
issue_body:
The `jsonschema` library that we use for validation requires a `FormatChecker` class to be passed to the validator in order to validate the `format` keyword: https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/37a7bb96cacb7db6e589bdb054e49e62c4367a7e/jsonschema/_format.py#L13-L15.

Invenio-Records currently does not support this: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records/blob/aab3b024d0078ccd36334e1d2faae02d078c8882/invenio_records/ext.py#L49-L57.

A quick fix would be to add `**kwargs` to https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records/blob/aab3b024d0078ccd36334e1d2faae02d078c8882/invenio_records/api.py#L78-L82 and the previous method, but I'm not sure if you'd like such a "liberal" solution.

issue_title: Compiler warnings about the visibility attribute from mingw
issue_body:
When attempting to cross-compile with mingw, I see a lot of "error: visibility attribute not supported in this configuration; ignored [-Werror=attributes]" messages (normally they're just warnings, but I use -Werror for CI builds).  See https://travis-ci.org/quixdb/squash/jobs/132697469#L1320 for an example.  I can reproduce on Fedora with Squash with something like `ming64-cmake . && make -j`.

If you want, you can adapt https://github.com/quixdb/squash/blob/26aa037b618c1a37d984099b8dd5a3e04d3058c3/squash/squash.h#L115

issue_title: Suppression of isolated ▁'s
issue_body:
To maximize likelihood, there are cases where subword tokens that are seen after a space (meaning the start of a "word") do not get the special underscore because they also appear in the middle of a character combination somewhere else.

Is it possible to suppress this behavior? Meaning, we don't want to have an isolated ▁ as part of the generated vocab list.

issue_title: Use of font-awesome icons in desktop applications / Icon naming
issue_body:
Opening an issue here in case someone is interested in using Font Awesome icons in desktop applications, they might want to have a look at [qtawesome](https://github.com/spyder-ide/qtawesome), which allows to use font awesome glyphs as standard QIcons in PyQt and PySide applications. 

```python
import qtawesome as qta
fa_icon = qta.icon('fa.flag')
fa_button = QtGui.QPushButton(fa_icon, 'Font Awesome!')
```
We support styling, stacking icons, and animations. Our primary motivation is to use Font Awesome for the icon set of the [Spyder IDE](https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder) (Spyder is a desktop IDE for the Python programming language). 
As we are working on the transition to font-awesome, we naturally missed a few icons, and I create a short list of glyphs to fill the gap. I know that you don't take PRs with icons, but I would be glad to contribute them, should you be interested.

Regarding the naming of icons, it could be interesting in the context of using font-awesome for desktop applications to either create aliases following the naming conventions from the [FreeDesktop specifications](http://freedesktop.org/wiki/Specifications/icon-naming-spec) or to create mapping to names following the freedesktop specifications. This could help defining creating standard themes for Qt / KDE based on font-awesome.

issue_title: Incorrect Mimetype/Content-Type Handling
issue_body:
I think there may be a bug in Content-Type/Mimetype handling.

If an operation has a single `produces` type defined in swagger and the implementation returns a flask Response object, both the swagger `produces` value and the Response object's `content-type` are returned. Example:

```yaml
operationId: app.get_greeting
produces:
  - text/plain
```

with the implementation:

```python
def post_greeting(name):
    response = flask.make_response('Hello {}\n'.format(name))
    response.headers['content-type'] = 'text/plain'
    response.mimetype = 'text/plain'
    return response
```

`curl -v` request to the route returns:

```
< Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
< Content-Length: 11
< Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
< Content-Length: 11
```

If you don't specifically set the Response object's content-type or mimetype, then the following is returned. 

```
< Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
< Content-Length: 11
< Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
< Content-Length: 11
```

This is a bigger issue when an operation has multiple `produces` values. If you use the same code as above with an added `application/json` to the `produces` values, `application/json` is always returned in addition to `text/plain`. 

```
< Content-Type: application/json
< Content-Length: 11
< Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
< Content-Length: 11
```

I suspect that the code causing this issue is in `connexion/decorator.py::BeginOfRequestLifecycleDecorator.get_response_container`.

issue_title: sessions not getting closed
issue_body:
we have noticed that use of this library opens new sessions at regular interval, and does not close it gracefully. even-though session.close() is getting called in __del__ method, but the issue is that method is used by garbage collector not guaranteed to be called, python community has preference over using rather contextManager so that it can be called via "with" to take care of closing of the session gracefully.

creating this issue to check if anybody has any workaround or suggestion and would be nice if someone can verify the behavior as well.

ps: Eventually the session might get closed by the server, but we want to be proactively closing the session once the interaction is over.

issue_title: DeprecationWarning: 'async' and 'await' will become reserved keywords in Python 3.7
issue_body:
It turns out that 'async' parameter which used in [a couple of methods](https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/search?utf8=✓&q=async&type=) prevents this library to be compatible with the future of Python. These warnings can be seen when running tests under Python 3.6 with `-Wd` option.

I guess this is not urgent since Python 3.7 is not released yet, but at the same time it affects public library API, so probably it makes sense to deprecate this parameter in advance and let developers fix their code.

issue_title: libgdynet linked to libdynet (causing double memory free)
issue_body:
This fix introduced a tricky error: https://github.com/clab/dynet/commit/06b1dd1b97f30feda05284d94554743eb058a286

Basically, `libdynet` should implement everything only on CPU, while `libgdynet` implements things compiled on CPU+GPU, and they are independent of each-other. (Once multi-device support is working properly, `libdynet` will be phased out, and only `libgdynet` will remain, with the option to choose CPU or GPU).

This fix inadvertently broke this independence, and now `libdynet` is linked by `libgdynet`. This is resulting in memory errors at the end of program execution because both libraries are trying to free the same memory. Normally this would be simple, but on mac OS it was a bit complicated because of a circular dependency between `libgdynet` (which implements main functionality) and `libdynetcuda` (which implements the GPU kernels).

As a proposed solution, I'd like to try to get rid of the `libdynetcuda` library, which contains only GPU code, and combine this with `libgdynet`. I've started doing this here, but it's still giving linker errors on boost: https://github.com/clab/dynet/tree/remove-dynetcuda
I may not have time to actually implement this today, so I'll post this here for now to keep people informed and allow anyone who's interested to take a look.

issue_title: nessrest doesn't use default /etc/ssl/certs ca bundle path
issue_body:
On debian/ubuntu, `/etc/ssl/certs` is a well known location where the systemwide ca bundle resides.

1. Should nessus use the default CA store?
1. Or should the user always explicitly supply it?

E.g. a nessus server is not commonly deployed using a public CA, but internal org CAs are often included in the OS-wide CA store.

OpenSSL and browsers are okay with the default store
```shell
openssl s_client -connect <fqdn>:8834 -verify_return_error
...
    Verify return code: 0 (ok)
```

nessrest isn't happy
```shell
ness_rest -c ness_rest_local.conf -p 10921 -t <target ip>
...
ssl.SSLError: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:600)
...
```

The following with `--ca_bundle` works
```
ness_rest -c ness_rest_local.conf -p 10921 -t <target ip> --ca_bundle /etc/ssl/certs
```

issue_title: BUG: netCDF4.Dataset arrays
issue_body: Bug might be a bit too harsh, but it should be noted that when using `netCDF4.Dataset` to read in data arrays, the array will not be a `numpy.ma.core.MaskedArray` when none of the values in the array are equal to the fill value, even if the variable has a `_FillValue` key (or equivalent) . Instead it will be read as a `numpy.ndarray`. This can cause issues when scripts depend on accessing the `mask` attribute of NumPy masked arrays. Should we make sure there is consistency in the 'data' keys of `Radar` and `Grid` objects? Could use the `isinstace()` built-in to check the type, and change accordingly.

issue_title: Make an example (and functionality?) for building wcs from stars world coordinates and pixel coordinates
issue_body:
This is obviously a big task with a lot of details, but prompted by @perrygreenfield's suggestion at the spectroscopy workshop, I thought it would be good to get it in here.

Basically, the following sort of thing should be possible:

>>> star_sc = SkyCoord(ra_stars, dec_stars) #lets not worry about how you decided which star is which
>>> star_pixels = np.array(x_stars, y_stars)
>>> wcs = gwcs.wcs_from_matches(star_sc, star_pixels)
>>> assert wcs.pixel_to_world(x_stars, ystars) == star_sc.ra, star_sc.dec # this isn't exactly right but hope gets the idea across

Does the basic task there make sense? Ideally this would be a part of a tutorial that combines with `photutils` or similar to show the real use case of "I have an image from a telescope with only very rough WCS, and want to get a better one by matching to something else".  Although that tutorial might be better in `astropy-tutorials` rather than in `gwcs` proper.

issue_body:
Current Pub / Sub API requires a topic to create a subscription. This is unnecessary and cumbersome.  It is also not feasible for some use cases: some users will only be given a subscription resource name to work with and not know the topic.

Current API: 

```python
from google.cloud import pubsub

client = pubsub.Client()
topic = client.topic(topic_name)
subscription = topic.subscription(sub_name)
```

Target:

```python
subscription = client.subscription(sub_name)
```

issue_title: pageSize vs. maxResults
issue_body:
What is the point of the two arguments? For old-style APIs (i.e. discovery based), JSON paging is done via `maxResults`.

For new-style APIs (i.e. those with gRPC support), paging is done via `pageSize` (both in JSON and gRPC).

Currently our iterator takes `maxResults` to mean a maximum **over many pages** while `pageSize` (at least in terms of the two english words put together) means **the size of one page**.
- Is it worth offering distinct `page_size` and `max_results` keyword params?
- @lukesneeringer, is there an equivalent concept of max results vs. page size in GAX?
- When no `pageSize` is used in a new-style API what happens? Some of our GAX helper docstrings say "If not passed, defaults to a value set by the API."

/cc @bjwatson @tseaver @jonparrott 

Relevant to https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/3270#discussion_r110059932

**NOTE**: This is copied mostly verbatim from an old email thread.

issue_title: BaseAWSObject has wrong regex for valid_names, specifically for SQS
issue_body:
From AWS:
Please enter a name for your new queue. Queue names must be 1-80 characters in length and be composed of alphanumeric characters, hyphens (-), and underscores (_). Your queue will be created in the US West (Oregon) region.

However, in BaseAWSObject:
valid_names = re.compile(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9]+$')

So using troposphere, I cannot include - or _ in my SQS names even though it's valid.

issue_title: using a PEX with pytest-xdist
issue_body:
Hi, 

I am trying to use a PEX to run tests from the pytest framework while using the pytest-xdist plugin for distributed testing. 

However I believe I am facing an issue similar to this [one](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pants-devel/-dRo4hEKNMc), as my worker processes are not recognizing packages that are packaged with pex.

My pex build is configured to have pytest as a module entry point (`-m pytest`) and from this main process I launch the workers in python subprocesses that are instantiated with the following command `pytest -d --tx popen//python=python3`.

When the workers begin executing I get the error `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named '_pytest'` which leads me to believe that the pytest package (contained in my pex file) is not being recognized by the subprocesses. 

I've tried to bootstrap the pex environment by configuring the workers before their execution in the `pytest_configure_node` xdist hook with a call to `pex_bootstrapper.bootstrap_pex_env('my.pex')` but the problem remains.

Any ideas? Thanks

issue_title: 1.0 release backwards incompatibilities
issue_body:
This is a proposed list of backwards incompatible changes that will be introduced in 1.0 and is subject to change.

For the pex tool:
  * pex -r will take requirements.txt instead of requirement specifiers (e.g. "Flask>=0.9")
  * requirement specifiers will be arguments to pex
  * Further pip-compatible flag aliases such as -f/--find-links will be added
  * --python will be used to specify an *exact* python shebang

For pex invocation:
  * PEX_INTERPRETER changed to accept interpreter class, e.g. 'CPython==2.7.9' or 'PyPy'
  * new variable PEX_REPL will be used to drop into a REPL
  * new variable PEX_PYTHON will be used to specify the exact interpreter to invoke and will override PEX_INTERPRETER

issue_title: nav-bar UX
issue_body:
We currently use a ['persistent' navigation drawer](https://material.io/guidelines/patterns/navigation-drawer.html#navigation-drawer-behavior) for tablet and desktop sizes.

However the guidelines say:

> Persistent navigation drawers are not recommended for apps with multiple levels of hierarchy that require using an up arrow for navigation.

issue_title: Aldryn News & Blog and Aldryn docs need to be brought into line
issue_body:
Forthcoming Aldryn docs will give this repository as an example of good practice in creating Addons. However, there are various places where this repository does not conform to good practices as outlined in the Aldryn documentation.

* [ ] http://docs.aldryn.com/en/latest/reference/addons/code-conventions.html#model-field-naming - we don't do this; see https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-newsblog/blob/develop/aldryn_newsblog/models.py#L109 for example. Should we try to maintain the conventions suggested in the documentation?
* [ ]  Aldryn Addons should provide "vanilla" frontend files, and not be locked to a particular Boilerplate whenever possible (see https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-docs/blob/restructure/docs/reference/addons/template-structure.rst#maintaining-compatibility-with-multiple-boilerplates) otherwise we risk a situation where some Addons only work with some Boilerplates. Aldryn News & Blog needs some plain frontend templates etc that will work well with the HTML5 Boilerplate.

'''yaml
"name": "kubernetes"

  restart and place containers on healthy nodes if a node ever goes away.

issue_title: GirderFileIdToVolume does not preserve server-side filename
issue_body: It writes the file to the volume and names it as the file ID, which removes the file extension. issue_title: Expand included features file when generating UFOs
issue_body:
When a source master UFO has a features.fea file with an `include` statement, it should be expanded into that file.
We can’t just rewrite the `include` statement with the relative path since the FDK semantics won’t allow that for more than one level deep.

issue_title: "data", "response" and "status_code" executor methods as attributes
issue_body:
This is a design decision. I propose changing "data", "response" and "status_code" executor methods to be accessed as attributes instead of methods.

Current behaviour:
``` python
>>> response.some_data().data()
"this is a raw data"
```

Proposed behaviour:
``` python
>>> response.some_data().data
"this is a raw data"
````

issue_title: Improve links to functions/attributes/enums in documentation by doing smarter replacements
issue_body:
We are changing extracted metadata to include the full symbol names for functions/enums/attributes rather than the names minus the prefixes. Once that's done, we can more effectively search/replace mention is the metadata documentation so we don't produce as many broken links.

Some discussion on the topic here:
https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/pull/584#discussion_r150613442

issue_title: Counter Resetting Every Read
issue_body:
I am using a USB-6009 multifunction DAQ with Windows 17 and Python 3.6 as a digital counter on the PFI0 port. The counter tests good in NI MAX. Also, if I read multiple samples, the counter does increment as expected when presented with a 1 kHz square wave. 

However, I find that the counter state is reset before every read operation. In other words, if I read 1 sample at a time, I will get the value "0" or "1" as an output. If I read, for example, 1000 samples at once, each sample (more or less, depending on sampling rate) is incrementing as expected. 

I am using the example code in ci_count_edges.py, which uses i_channels.add_ci_count_edges_chan().

The underlying NI DAQ MX function is DAQmxReadCounterU32Ex(), which isn't supposed to do this. Therefore, the cause is a bit of a mystery to me.

issue_title: Scrape all ECF RSS feeds and incorporate them into RECAP database
issue_body: There are 173 ECF instances with RSS feeds, according to https://www.pacer.gov/psco/cgi-bin/links.pl Most of them have a 24-hour lookback, and have varying scopes: some include all docket entries, others include only court orders, etc. In any case, they provide information about newly filed information and docket metadata that should be incorporated into the RECAP database. A scraper should run (ideally coincident with the ~20-minute update frequency of these feeds, but certainly no less than 2x/ the expiry frequency (24hrs/2 -> 12hours) to scrape all of this data. issue_title: programmatic creation of personal endpoints
issue_body:
I've been writing instructions for users to download the globuspersonalconnect client to create an endpoint for a specific folder, and I think this is reasonable given that they need to open up the browser and log in, etc. However I'm looking for a solution that is many fewer steps than what I'm figuring out to do. I am doing the following:

 - define computer as personal endpoint
 - edit config-paths to limit permissions 
 - edit (something else I haven't found yet) to define a shared folder to be where the images are.

I felt ok about 3 steps, but when I did a list (and then went to the web interface) I saw...

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/34441695-2f6aabc2-ec72-11e7-8d1f-ce943a4004a2.png)

Absolutely nothing! The reason of course being because the folder is hidden and I'd need to select to show it:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/34441704-4b367a7a-ec72-11e7-86ec-d9aeff2f78f8.png)

But I think we can do better than this, because each step that we add on to the flow the more likely it is that the user is going to miss something and find it hard to use. Here is sort of what I'd like to do (and please let me know if this is reasonable).

## Endpoint Setup
The user has some (short) series of steps to set up the endpoint locally. Ideally, we could do this completely programatically (from within python) without needing to install "globuspersonalconnect" and "globus-cli" and still "globus-python-sdk" - that's a ton of dependencies for just this one thing! If I implemented it with the sregistry client, the logic would work as follows:

  1. The user creates the endpoint (or has sregistry do it for him/her) and adds the id to a variable that sregistry can access.
  2. When the client starts, the endpoint is found via the variable, and then the client finishes setting it up. This means **1)** creating a specific share for the sregistry storage folder (if it doesn't exist) and **2)** ensuring that the shared folder is by default not hidden (because by default it is hidden, and if I do a list on my command line I see nothing akin to the web interface.

This series of steps to read in the endpoint id and then check for paths and sharing should be the minimal requirement for setup. You can imagine if something is off the user can be notified immediately, and the functions that the user is given can be determined based on what is found.

Could you point me to the right docs / or make a suggestion about how to best accomplish this? I poked through the python sdk and the online docs but primarily see stuffs related to clients for transfer / server endpoints (as opposed to set up stuffs). Thank you!

issue_title: Add type hints
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Python's dynamic-typing nature makes it impossible to perform static type checking

### How can it be fixed?

We can add type hints (like in https://github.com/ethereum/ethereum-keys/pull/19) and use mypy to perform static type checking, and hopefully catch bugs before they affect our users. Just like in the PR above, pyannotate can be used to generate initial type hints

issue_title: Allow SimpleMRS to read from noisy input
issue_body:
The `-q` option is currently used in pipelines involving ACE and pyDelphin to suppress the input sentence from the output stream, which (along with `-T`, which suppresses derivations) allows pyDelphin to read the stream containing only MRS data. This option may not be supported in the future, so perhaps the SimpleMRS reader should allow noisy data (see how the [Penman](https://github.com/goodmami/penman) package [handles noisy data](https://github.com/goodmami/penman/blob/b7ecd64950b1cc7bef7e67eb7cc3b5391a5417b1/penman.py#L200-L223)).

Non-MRS data can probably be discarded. Alternatively, if we expect `SENT: ...` to contain the input string, we could use that to fill the XMRS object's `surface` field.

(edited to fix Penman URL)

issue_title: suggestion: build should install into a clean dedicated directory
issue_body:
this is a suggestion: the current situation that it is recommended to install into: PREFIX
see: http://docs.continuum.io/conda/build.html 
`PREFIX    build prefix where build script should install to`
is maybe not the best solution as this is also used to fetch the build dependencies into.

I would suggest to install into a clean dir: e.g. 

```
env/_build      (build PREFIX where build dependencies will be fetch into`)
env/_installdir      (build INSTALLDIR where build script should install to`)
```

the problem with the current situation is: that if one wants to compile something like e.g. make, chrpath patchelf, gcc, glibc ect  and one wants to define the same as a dependency one ends up with a mess and mostly missing bits.

```
package:
    name: "make"

requirements:
  build:
    - "make"
```

1 becauce the build dependency is first fetched into `env/_build` 
2 the resulting make is also installed into `env/_build` 
3 the final conda pkg is the difference in files
4 one ends up with nearly nothing: the info folder

because of this:  I suggest to install into a dedicated separate folder

I know there are ways around but it makes sense:
things like  calculating any differences ect fall away.

But who know I do not know conda so well inside and I might miss something: for linux I will implemented something like this in my fork

issue_title: clean up cli import in conda 4.3.x
Activate conda env by a path to the env
issue_body:
Hi. I am a PyCharm developer.

We already have support for conda in our IDE. Now we are doing automated activation of conda env in the terminal inside the IDE. And struggling with that.
Currently, at least as far as I see from the documentation, it is possible to activate an env by it's name. For automation purposes sometimes it is very inconvenient because what we have sometimes is only a path to the env and there is no reliable way to get a name from the path.

There is no such problem with virtualenv, which activates the same env where activate script belongs, which is not the case with conda.

Could you please provide a way to activate a conda env by it's path or point me to such feature if it already exists?

issue_title: update_dependencies flag applies to non-dependencies
issue_body:
I'm facing a problem with the update_dependencies option.

On the one hand, when running "conda install ..." I've noticed that conda tries to upgrade packages that I didn't ask it to upgrade (explicitly or implicitly). This is problematic because I don't necessarily want every installed package upgraded to the latest version every time I run "install", because my code may not be compatible w/ that version yet. The only solution for this that I know of is to set update_dependencies = False.

On the other hand, suppose I have version 2 of B installed, and then I run "conda install A==10" where version 10 of A depends on version 3 of B. I would expect that B is at that point upgraded. And it is, as long as update_dependencies = True.

So, it seems like I have to choose between one set of desired behavior or the other. 

Ideally, conda would first crawl the dependency tree for what I'm asking it to install. If a package lies outside of that tree, conda would try to keep it on the same version. If a package lies inside of that tree, conda would either keep that package on the same version (update_dependencies = False) or remove the current version from the spec (update_dependencies = True). I think the problem with the existing code is that it incorrectly assumes all installed packages to be in the dependency tree.

Here's the relevant section of code https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L682

issue_title: Does add_config support generic type time?
issue_body:
Hello VUnit Devs,

I'm trying to automate a testbench where I have several generics of type time. Is it possible to handle this generic type using add_config and dict in some kind of VHDL style? E.g.

`testbench.add_config(name="example", generics=dict(SIZE=1000, EX_TIME=???))`

I tried using a string for the time content, e.g. `EX_TIME="0.1 us"` but this is not working. When using simple integers, e.g. `EX_TIME=5` the content is interpreted with the simulation resolution (which is some kind of expected I think).

So the question is, if there is a more VHDL kind of time representation which can be used for the different configurations? This would absolutely improve the readability of the python run script.

Thanks for the support and keep on with the great VUnit work!

Best Regards,
Tobi

### Description

Right now, I've got automation for the entire process creating numerous rules with az network nsg create (and similar with xplat cli), but it's very slow - and parallelizing (at least with xplat) doesn't work right - the updates seem to stomp on each other.

I'm wondering if there is support for anything equivalent to the opposite of 'az network nsg rule list'? (I'm fine with it being a little raw, but would rather not have to do the underlying API support directly in my scripts at this time.) 

If there isn't, consider this a feature request. I'd love to see something like:

'az network nsg rule import [--replace] [--update]  --json file.json' with the nsg name, resource group name, etc. options on command line acting as defaults if they were not included in the content. Input format of the json would be identical to the output format of nsg rule list. --replace would request a wholesale replacement of the ruleset (delete all not present), --update would load, and replace any that had the same index and/or id.

issue_title: Move Cronbox out of Chef into Lambda
issue_body:
Currently, we are running a dedicated EC2 instance configured with Chef for Cron only. To complete our move to Docker, we want to get this functionality out of system-level Cron and into scheduled Cloudwatch events. There are two kinds of scheduled tasks:

- Dotmap updates, zip collections, and other big tasks are implemented as whole-instance processes configured via EC2 user data (in #614 we switched to Docker for these). They are kicked off via `openaddr-run-ec2-command` configures and starts a single-purpose EC2 instance.
- Coverage page is updated hourly with a task that does not run on a dedicated instance, but instead is called as `openaddr-sum-up-data` directly. This can’t be run on Lambda itself unless we drastically change the installation process for OpenAddresses, and its hourly run is too frequent to make economic sense on EC2.

The first group is pretty easy, and can be run easily and directly from Lambda if appropriate IAM role permissions are in place. The Coverage data may be more complex, and could be handled as a dedicated instance if run less frequently, e.g. daily.

async def something():

```

aiohttp 2.2.0 on Python 3.5.3

issue_title: Option for removing the New->Terminal menu entry.
issue_body:
There should be an official option for removing the New->Terminal menu entry.

This has been asked many times before, such as in https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyterhub/issues/1195, but also on StackOverflow and in other forums. The answer always get dismissed with the argument that people can execute code in the notebook anyway, or shell out a terminal command. That is quite valid when the kernel is run on the same host, or in the same container, as the Jupyter Notebook web interface, but when you are running a kernel on a remote host using the ``kernel_gateway`` and ``nb2kg`` packages, or enterprise kernel gateway, the terminal would be created on a different system to where the kernel is running.

In this case of using a kernel gateway, you don't necessarily want people to be able to create the terminal or even see the menu item, because it will run on the system where the Jupyter Notebook web interface is running, not where the kernel is running. You may want to prevent this, leaving the only place a user can run code being where the kernel is running.

The suggestion of uninstalling ``terminado`` package after it has been installed, or setting ``SHELL`` to ``/bin/false`` are both clunky solutions and don't get rid of the menu entry.

issue_title: Add a flag to the match function which indicates if you want your callback called for matches, non-matches or both
issue_body:
When matching a lot of files against a lot of files with yara python , the callback function gets called every time , match or no match , which creates a lot of overhead if you're only interested in the matches.
Would it be possible to provide an option where you can specify whether you want your callback called for matches, non-matches or both? I think performance could increase a lot ...

issue_title: Start a production subdomain registrar node
issue_body:
In order to ship subdomain support in the browser, we'd need to spin up a production subdomain registrar node that registers subdomains for a given domain.

We need to:

1. Figure out what domain to use.
2. Start a blockstack-core node which can submit updates on behalf of that domain.
3. Point a subdomain registrar at that blockstack-core node, and perform _some_ kind of spam protection.

For the spam protection, we could read the submitted zonefile, lookup the associated profile object, and require a valid social proof (which raises a related issue of whether or not social proofs will be valid with subdomain strings `foo.bar.id`).

Tagging @muneeb-ali @jcnelson for thoughts on spam control.

issue_title: Enhance extras.image to accept a path/URL
issue_body: Feature enhancement: enhance extras.image to accept a path/URL as an alternative to a base64 encoded PNG. Implementation: check the content type of the extra content. If it is not base64 then just assume it to be a path/URL. The resulting href/src for the should be the provided string value as-is (optionally: could check if path starts with file: or http(s)) Background: In my setup, I do not have base64 encoded extra content when the report gets generated. Instead, my test logic already creates an external folder where screenshots are stored during test execution. So essentially I'd like to just link to them using a given file:// or relative URL inside the report, no matter if the report is self-contained or not. e.g. this ``` extra.append(pytest_html.extras.image('../screenshots/foo_bar.png')) extra.append(pytest_html.extras.image('file:///screenshots/foo_bar.png')) extra.append(pytest_html.extras.image('http://foo.bar/screenshots/foo_bar.png')) ``` would result in 3 tags in the report with their src/href set to the above values.

issue_title: General krb5 functions
issue_body:
My project [python-krbcontext](https://github.com/krbcontext/krbcontext/) relies on python-krbV heavily, and I've been told and suggest to choose python-gssapi, since it's been being developed actively, is compatible with Python 3, and a strong reason is gssapi can do everything krbV does. I have a quick look at gssapi and realize that it implements GSSAPI spec, whereas krbV handles `context`, `ccache`, `principal`, and `keytab` with lower level krb5 functions. So, I would like to ask if gssapi has equivalent functionalists, such as
- initialize `ccache` in either default or user-specified place
- read `keytab` from either default or user-specified place
- read credential cache info from a `ccache`, either the default or user-specified

My project actually doesn't require GSSAPI, because `krbcontext` works in the client side of a GSSAPI context, and it is designed to happen before client side code start to call gssapi APIs.

issue_title: In shipping address checkout view, "addressbook" should be two words
issue_body: The shipping_address template has "An address from your addressbook?" as the header above the user's address book. "addressbook" should be changed to "address book".

issue_title: Process pipes aren't garbage collected
issue_body:
# Process pipes aren't garbage collected
# General
When using redirection for StandardOutput (and possibly StandardError, StandardInput as well) the pipes aren't garbage collected when the Process reference is collected.

The following silly piece of code lives (probably) forever in mono, but crashes in dotnet after 217 iterations on my machine:
```
public static void Main() {
            for (int i = 0; i < int.MaxValue; i++) {
                Process.Start(new ProcessStartInfo {
                    FileName = "ls",
                    UseShellExecute = false,
                    RedirectStandardOutput = true
                });
                Console.WriteLine(i);
            }
}
```
Adding a ```.StandardOutput.Close()``` extends lifetime 5 fold, indicating other things are not being collected.
